titleAr: يا لهف نفسي على صخر وقد لهفت
titleEn: My soul aches for Sakhr

verses.sadr: يا لهف نفسي على صخر وقد لهفت
ابكي المهين تلاد المال ان نزلت

translationEn: O how my soul aches for Sakhr — though it ached before! And will my lamenting cure this madness of the heart? Pour upon him tears that never run dry, and weep for your brother each dawn you wake among them. Weep for the one who shielded inherited wealth when bitter cold descended, crushing the people with its harsh grip. And weep for your brother from time grown familiar — for time, woe unto you, is full of calamity and ruin.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: anguish, heartache, longing grief
madness, distraction of the mind
my lamenting, my anguished grief
drained, exhausted (of tears)
ancestral wealth, inherited property
bitter cold (lit. white-grey, describing frost)
ruin, destruction, stripping bare

context: A brief but intense elegy in which Al-Khansa mourns her brother Sakhr, calling on herself to weep without cease and cataloguing what has been lost with him — his protection of wealth, his generosity, and his role as shield against the cruelty of time.

titleEn: My eye is sore from weeping
translationEn: My eye is sore and inflamed, weeping for Sakhr with unceasing flow — tears from my eye pour down my cheek and over my hand. The pupil of my eye floods with stream after stream. My soul, after Sakhr, acknowledges its own destruction. In my soul there remains something of Sakhr that cannot be described in words. My soul is burdened with sorrows, burning and grieving. With the remembrance of Sakhr my soul is struck down anew each day. Sakhr was a fortress and a guardian for the weak, a refuge and a spring of life for the aged senile woman. When the north wind or the south wind blew in fury, he would slaughter the choice fat camels and the young she-camels with their milk. He filled the great bowl with fat until its rim was blackened with soot in the dark, and you saw the hungry pressing close to it and coming eagerly. You saw hands plunging in, greasy and dripping, coming and going like sand-grouse flocking — like east wind and north wind over brimming water-troughs. Now that Sakhr has made me drink bitterness, my life has become narrow and constrained. It had been, for a time, a blooming meadow freshly visited.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: became sore and inflamed (of the eye from weeping)
pupil of the eye
acknowledging its own destruction/death
struck down, overwhelmed, exhausted
the choice fat camels selected for slaughter
large bowl/cauldron for feeding guests
freshly visited, newly blossoming (of a meadow)

context: An extended elegy in a short meter (possibly mujtath or mujzaʾ), mourning Sakhr with vivid imagery of his hospitality — the slaughtering of camels, the great feast-bowl, and guests coming and going — contrasted with the poet's present grief and narrowed life.

titleEn: Pour your tears or find patience

verses.sadr: ألا يا لهف نفسي بعد عيش

verses.ajuz: واذ فينا معاوية بن عمر و

translationEn: Shed your tears and pour them out, or find composure — be patient if you can, but you never can. Say: the best of Banu Sulaym and their horseman of the plain of al-ʿAqiq has gone. My weeping after Sakhr is like one who walks the wrong road away from her destination. By your father, my breast has not been stripped of it by any disgrace I committed nor by disobedience. But I found patience to be better than sandal-removal and a shaved head in mourning. Will those nights ever return to us, and those days we spent at the hills of al-Shaqiq? Oh how my soul aches for the life we had at the meadow of al-Mukhtam and al-Madiq! When among us were horsemen of every battle alarm, when they rushed out — and bold young men of the desert gaps. When war clashed its molars and the brave warriors met at the lightning-flash of blades. And when among us was Muʿāwiya ibn ʿAmr, riding a white she-camel like a fine bull-camel. Weep for him, for he has departed praised, sound in counsel, admired by his friends. He is the plain calamity — not a faint-heart — great in judgment, patient in the face of clamor.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: pour out, shed (tears)
away from the straight path, astray
filial disobedience, ingratitude
sandal-removal and shaving the head (mourning rites)
its molars — clash of war's fangs, the height of battle
the brave warriors, heroes
the clear calamity, the manifest loss

context: Al-Khansa addresses herself, struggling between uncontrollable grief and the knowledge that patience is nobler than public mourning rites. She elegizes both Sakhr and Muʿāwiya ibn ʿAmr, recalling happier days of tribal strength.

titleAr: يا عين جودي بدمع منك مهراق
titleEn: O eye, pour out your tears

verses.sadr: يا عين جودي بدمع منك مهراق
verses.ajuz: اذا هدى الناس أو هموا باطراق
ما زلت في كل امساء واشراق

translationEn: O my eye, be generous with your outpoured tears whenever people guide their way or bow their heads in thought. The remembrance of Sakhr comes over me whenever a ringed dove coos upon the branches. And every tearful woman who spends the night wakeful weeps the weeping of a heart heavy with grief and longing. Be not deceived — death cuts down all of creation except the One who remains. You were the noble youth, the protector of what was dear, giving abundantly with a face bright and radiant. You gave the old she-camel and the young one together, and every swift horse racing toward glory. I shall keep lamenting Abā Ḥassān in my grief, every evening and every morning without cease.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: poured out, spilled (of tears)
a cooing dove with neck-rings (collared dove)
cooed, made her repetitive call (of a dove)
a tearful woman, one overcome by weeping
cutting down, taking away (said of death)
radiant, bright, shining (of a face)
an old she-camel (lit. the fang)

context: Al-Khansa uses the dove's cooing as a trigger for her grief, a classical topos in Arabic elegy. The poem praises Sakhr's generosity with camels and horses while swearing to mourn him at every dawn and dusk.

titleEn: Events of time make your eye flow

verses.ajuz: تبكي على صخر وفي الدهر مذهل
الا من لعين لا تجف دموعها
له سورة في قومه ما تحول

verses.sadr: من المجد إلا حيث ما نلت أطول
ولا بلغ المهدون في القول مدحة
verses.ajuz: ولا صدقوا إلا الذي فيك أفضل
وما الغيث في جعد الثرى دمث الربى
تعم بها بل سيب كفيك أجزل

verses.sadr: من الضيم لا يؤذى ولا يتذلل
له في عرين الغيل عرس واشبل

verses.ajuz: أخو الجود معروف له الجود والندى

translationEn: Is it from the events of time that your eye flows, weeping for Sakhr — and in time there is much to stun the mind? Is there anyone whose tears do not dry — whenever you say they have subsided, they pour forth and overflow again? For a noble man of immense generosity and excellence, whose high standing among his people never waned. No hand reaching for glory ever attained as much as you reached — and you reached highest. Nor did eulogizers in their speech achieve a true praise except what falls short of your greater excellence. Not even the rain falling on soft, loamy hills where the pouring cloud bursts open is as wide in giving as your two hands, and your bounty is more lavish. Your neighbor is protected, secure on a height, shielded from injustice — never harmed and never humbled. A man of the people whose tent is crowded, who when unjustly provoked is like a lurking lion gathering itself for attack. With powerful blunt fingertips, a mighty warrior — he has in the lion's thicket a mate and cubs. A fierce lion with a wide gaping jaw, a jungle predator, fearsome to face, with wide wide-set eyes. A companion of generosity — generosity and liberality are his sworn allies as long as Taʿār and Yadhbul stand.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: flows, pours (of tears from the eye)
generosity, the gift of abundant giving
high standing, prestige, power
the pouring, streaming heavy rain
flowing bounty, generosity
a lurking lion crouching ready to spring
wide-jawed, with a great gaping mouth (of a lion)

context: An elaborate elegy comparing Sakhr's generosity to rain and his ferocity to a lion in his thicket. Al-Khansa argues that no praise can fully capture him and that his neighbor was always safe under his protection.

titleEn: O my two eyes, weep and wail

verses.sadr: على صخر بن عمر و إن هذا

translationEn: O my two eyes, woe to you both — pour out tears, abundant and unsparing, and raise your lament! Be generous with tears other than your own, for you have already bequeathed me grief and humiliation. Weep for Sakhr the radiant, father of orphans, who bore every stumble and burden of the helpless. If you aid me, then assist me with tears that drench both cheeks. Weep for Sakhr ibn ʿAmr — though indeed your ocean has run low and diminished — with a burning pain settled in the ribs. You have already left me grief and humiliation, and a burning sorrow lodged within. Rise, O Ṣafiyya, among the women in the burning midday sun — let them not seek shade — tearing their garments, and every face is little enough to burn and blister for him.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: pour out (dual imperative), let your tears flow
scant, miserly (of tears)
the radiant, the glorious one (epithet of praise)
a stumble, a slip — a burden borne for others
to drench, to wet thoroughly
has dwindled, faded, been exhausted
tearing their collars/garments (mourning gesture)

context: Al-Khansa calls on her two eyes and on a woman named Ṣafiyya to join her in public mourning for Sakhr, describing him as the father of orphans and bearer of others' burdens. The poem features the pre-Islamic mourning custom of women going into the sun and tearing their garments.

titleEn: My eye wept and its wailing was right

verses.sadr: معاوية بن عمر و كان ركني
فبكي أم عمر و كل يوم

translationEn: My eye wept — and rightly so was its wailing — for the great calamity has broken my two wings. I lost forever — how has time gnawed my support? — for people whose love is little. They were my two wings when I encountered them, greeting them with warm welcome. My brother reminded me of people who have gone, bringing their memory upon me whenever anyone speaks. Muʿāwiya ibn ʿAmr was my pillar, and Sakhr was their dense cooling shade. I remembered them and grief overcame me and wounded my heart, and upon my people fell long sorrow. People of might — as though they were ever in proud anger — and of glory extended by long noble lineage. They led Maʿad in their youth, and they led when they were young men or mature men of middle age. So let Umm ʿAmr weep every day for a trusted brother whose face was beautiful.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the wail, the loud lamentation
broke, fractured (re-breaking a mended bone)
the great calamity, the momentous event
my two wings — her two brothers as her support
warm welcome, acceptance (also the east wind)
the dense, deeply cooling shade
Maʿad — a major Arab tribal grouping, ancestor of northern Arabs

context: Al-Khansa mourns both her brothers, Sakhr and Muʿāwiya ibn ʿAmr, describing them as her two wings and the twin pillars of their tribe. The poem reflects her sense of complete vulnerability after losing both brothers.

titleAr: آلا ليت أمي لم تلدني سوية
titleEn: Would my mother had never borne me

verses.sadr: آلا ليت أمي لم تلدني سوية
نعى ما ابن عمر و اثكلته هوابلي
verses.ajuz: فقلت له : ماذا تقول ؟ فقال لي :

translationEn: Would that my mother had never borne me whole, and that I had been dust in the midwives' hands. And would that the sky had fallen and covered the earth, and every rough-shod and sandal-wearing person had died all together — on the morning when a bearer of news of Sakhr came to me and terrified me, bequeathing me a grief of long-lingering anguish. I said to him: What do you say? He said to me: He brings news of the death of Ibn ʿAmr — my bereavements have bereaved him too. Since then I find no pleasure in any blessing or life, nor do I weep at the call of any bereaved mother. Let death now pursue my kinsfolk after him, giving them sip after sip, one affliction following another.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: whole, sound, fully formed
midwives
a bearer of news of death, one who announces a loss
terrified me, filled me with dread
anxieties, lingering sorrows, worries of the heart
my bereavements, my calamities
one who has just drunk — the first drinker at a watering

context: One of Al-Khansa's most desolate elegies, wishing she had never been born and that the world had ended the day she received news of Sakhr's death. She narrates the moment of receiving the news in direct speech.

titleEn: O eye, be generous with flowing tears

verses.ajuz: و ابكي على صخر بدمع همول

verses.sadr: فليس ذا يا عين وقت الخذول
بل عنده من نابه في فضول

translationEn: O my eye, be generous with your flowing tears, and weep for Sakhr with tears that pour in torrents. Do not fail me when weeping is earnest — this is no time, O eye, for failing. Weep for Abā Ḥassān and shed tears for the handsome man, so hospitable and promising. A fine winter companion when widows of the tribe gathered around him on cold damp mornings, coming to him seeking his protection, raising their calls of distress in the encampment. What an excellent neighbor of the people in times of hardship, when people take refuge with a neighbor who is weak and worthless! His face itself guided people to his generosity — blessed was the one who guided another to him. He did not confine his bounty to himself alone; at his side was excess for whoever came to him in need. People recognized in him a high standing — a generous man who was no weakling. His giving was lavish and his charges against enemies were the charges of a great chief against chiefs equally fierce. His counsel was wisdom and in his speech were admonitions that cured the burning pain of inner grief. He was no noble horse that refuses its back to bear heavy burdens, nor one who coughs when asked — for the chest of a cougher is too cramped for generosity. Time has struck me with terror — woe to it! — for it has taken from me the horseman of horsemen and the great warrior. You have left me in the midst of Banu ʿIlla, moving among them like an outcast stranger.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: flowing, pouring (of tears)
torrential, ceaselessly flowing (of tears)
one who fails, abandons, lets down
clinging, seeking protection, holding fast
cries of distress, laments
the burning pain of inner grief, an unquenched ache
a great strong warrior, a mighty hero

context: A long, detailed elegy praising Sakhr's multiple virtues: his hospitality to widows in winter, his generosity, his wisdom in speech, his ferocity in battle, and his neighborly protection. Al-Khansa ends with her sense of displacement after his death.

titleEn: The canopy of Abu Hassan has fallen
translationEn: Truly Abā Ḥassān was a canopy that has fallen — one of what God built, providing dense shade. He was long-necked and tall, never overcome by his equal, his thoughts composed, great in stature. You would think him angry from his dignity — that is a nature in him that never changes. Woe to his mother! He was a kindler of war when cast into it as an armored horseman. The fat camels suffered at his cookpot, and the old she-camel and the unbroken warrior-beast. Where shall I find a horseman to ride out with at dawn as your equal, when burdens fall upon me? You have left me, O Sakhr, among young men, as though after you I am a stranger among them.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a canopy/throne that has collapsed (metaphor for a great man's fall)
deeply shading, giving dense shade
long-necked and tall (of stature and pride)
a kindler of war, one who ignites battle
one wearing a coat of mail, armored
the large fat she-camels (slaughtered for feasts)
a stranger, one transplanted among others

context: A compact elegy using the image of a fallen canopy to capture Sakhr's role as shelter and protector. Al-Khansa praises his commanding presence and his generosity in slaughtering camels, ending with her loneliness among young men who cannot replace him.

titleEn: I weep for the hero Sakhr
translationEn: I weep for the hero upon whom you have heaped heavy stones — Sakhr. He rode girt with his sword, planting his spear time and again. O Sakhr, who now will stand for the horses when their riders are turned back in haste? They wore coats of iron chain-mail — you would think them camels (so many) when around him. Woe is me for you when the cold north wind blows and the damp cold hill yields nothing but a sparse drizzle — to strike fear into those people whom we count as dependents among us. In giving hospitality, the best of all creation is Sakhr, and the noblest of them in deed. He is the one hoped for, the one sought out, and the most abundant in bestowing gifts.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: you covered, you heaped upon (with earth and stones)
heavy loads, heavy stones (of the grave)
girded, belted (for battle)
clothed in rings of iron — wearing chain-mail
the damp cold cloud/hill that yields only sparse drizzle
dependents, those under one's care
gifts, bounty bestowed

context: A poem in mujzaʾ al-kāmil meter mourning Sakhr, mixing images of his battle prowess — iron-clad horsemen, his planted spear — with his role as provider in cold winter, contrasting the bleakness of the present with his former warmth.

titleEn: O my eye, overflow and be not stingy
translationEn: O my eye, overflow and be not stingy — for you have not yet spent your tears freely enough. Be generous with your weeping and let it gush like a channel swept by a rushing stream. Weep for the best of those whom wailers lament, the powerful chief, the most excellent. He was long-sworded and lofty in purpose, no lowly wretch nor weakling. A protector of what was dear, who never shrank back from the clash of battle in the heat of the noonday sun. When he appeared, enemies feared him as a watering lion, father of cubs. A proud lion with a mane, who guarded the thicket and would not be driven from it. Whenever he traced his lineage he defended it and protected it by his lofty, towering glory. He defended the tribe on the day of hardship, and the neighbor, the guest, and those seeking shelter. And a mare running like the rushing channel, her mighty chest boiling like a cauldron — I reined her in among the early ancestors. Let the winter's dependents weep for you when the she-camels pressed close in shelter from the north wind.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: overflow, pour out (imperative)
the sword-belt — length of the sword-belt indicates height and rank
to lean into the clash, to thrust into battle
a lion coming to water, a fiercely advancing lion
possessing a mane (of a lion), mighty-maned
a mare whose chest overflows with power and energy
the north wind, cold northern wind

context: Al-Khansa urges her eye to pour tears for Sakhr, praising his military ferocity through the lion metaphor and his generosity to guests and dependents in winter. The poem ends with an image of the she-camels huddling from the north wind — who will shelter them now?

titleAr: ألا يا صخر إن أبكيت عيني
titleEn: O Sakhr, you made me weep

verses.sadr: ألا يا صخر إن أبكيت عيني
و كنت احق من ابدى العويلا

translationEn: O Sakhr — though you have made my eye weep — you had for a long time made me laugh. I wept for you among women wailing loudly, and you were the most deserving of those who display their lament. With you alive I repelled the great calamity — now who will repel the immense affliction? When weeping over a slain man seems shameful, I find your weeping to be fair and beautiful.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: you made me weep, you caused my tears
you made me laugh, you brought me joy
women wailing, lamenting loudly
the wail, the loud mourning cry
the great, the immense (calamity)
the affliction, the grave matter, the calamity

context: A brief but powerful elegy in which Al-Khansa balances past joy with present grief, noting that Sakhr had been her source of laughter for a long time before becoming the cause of her tears. The final verse is a striking reversal: weeping for him is always beautiful.

titleEn: What ails your eye, what is wrong with it

verses.ajuz: ابعد ابن عمر و من آل الشريد م
كفاها ابن عمر و ولم يستعن

translationEn: What is wrong with your eye — what ails it? Tears have soaked its garment through. After Ibn ʿAmr of the family of al-Sharīd, the earth has been weighed down with its burdens. I swore I would grieve for the one who has perished, and ask every weeping woman what is wrong with her. By your father's life, what a fine youth — with him war kindles its embers. Sharp of spear and keen of tongue, repaying every slanderer in kind. I resolved every resolution about my own soul — let it be woe to my soul, woe to it. I shall burden my soul with effort — either against it or for it. If the soul is patient it finds ease; if the soul is impatient it is only the more wretched. We dishonor our souls, but dishonoring one's soul in the day of battle keeps it longer. We know that the deaths of men reach wherever fate decrees. Let death drag after the young man, dragging its train, leaving them routed and scattered. And a swaying battle line, above which helmets gleam doubled in rows — like a rain-cloud with its massed ridge, shooting at the clouds and shot at in return. And horses pressing forward crowded with armored men, engaging heroes with the sword. And a verse like the edge of a lance-point that will last while its speaker perishes. It cuts through the locks of Yadhbul, refusing to abandon its mountain goats. Ibn ʿAmr, you spoke it and made it easy — and no man spoke its like. And if a single moment brought him to his end, he had many a time multiplied his killings before. The high mountains fell when he was killed, and the earth shook its shaking. The stars shifted from his loss, and the sun veiled itself in its veils. A calamity brought on by a criminal — the nursing mothers are betrayed of their burdens. Ibn ʿAmr dealt with it alone, not seeking help — and if it had been anyone else it would have been beyond them. He is not the first, but he will spare the tribe whatever afflicts it. In a narrow place of battle, death drags its hem — he thrust at it and when it turned back he wetted its haunches with blood. White-sword women he protected at the noonday heat, lifting their hems in alarm. A she-camel he drove, given while seated, he branded her unbranded parts with the sword. A swift she-camel like a wild-ass of the rugged ground, he cut her joints in the pass — that was her portion for him. He grants his horses the enemy's land and hurls his warriors into the raid, casting away their children. And a lament he raised like the lament of the lion's den — the eye recognized its cubs.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: her garment, her robe — here the skin around the eye
its embers, its firebrands (of war)
the slanderer, one who speaks evil
the hated thing — the day of battle, the day of hardship
a cloud with a massed thunderhead ridge
the mountain goats — metaphor for high, inaccessible subjects
unbranded spots — places on a camel not yet marked

context: One of the longest elegies in this batch, with 31 verses, this poem moves through grief, self-exhortation to patience, cosmic imagery of mountains falling and stars shifting at Sakhr's death, and then an extended sequence praising his battle deeds and generosity, ending with a lioness-and-cubs image.

titleEn: When I saw the full moon eclipse

verses.ajuz: رنينا وما يغني الرنين وقد اتى

verses.sadr: لقد خار مرداسا على الناس قاتله
وقد منع الشفاء من هو نائله

verses.ajuz: وان كل هم همه فهو فاعله
وان كل واد يكره الناس هبطه
متى ما توازن ماجدا يعتدل به ،

translationEn: When I saw the full moon darken and eclipse, the stars of its midst and its flowing streams wailing — I wailed too, though wailing avails nothing, for the news of your death came from the direction of the village, borne by its carrier. How his killer chose poorly against Mirdās — even though his sisters-in-law and his wives surrounded him. His family said: Is there a cure to be found for him? — and the cure had been withheld from the one who would have attained it. The excellence of Mirdās over all people is his forbearance, and whatever matter he resolved himself to, he would carry out. Every valley that people feared to enter — you entered it, with a watering-place that you yourself drank from. You left behind you a long night and a dwelling, wolves trotting along the road's back. A captive like oryx of the barren plain you left, cowering among the dwellings, its tendons slack. You returned to them after your hardship more blessed — all of them bound to you and maintained by you. Whenever a noble man is weighed against him he is found balanced — as a hand balances the scale with its weight.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the full moon darkened in eclipse — cosmic image of loss
the stars on the edges of the lunar body
failed, chose badly, was weak
his wives (singular: حليلة)
a watering-place, a source of water
wolves (singular: عاسل) — those who roam by night
its weight, the counterweight on a scale

context: This elegy mourns Mirdās (a name for Sakhr or a companion), using the image of a lunar eclipse as a cosmic sign of his death. Al-Khansa praises his forbearance and daring to enter feared valleys, and his return to his people with generosity despite hardship.

titleEn: May rain water the grave beyond Ghamra

verses.sadr: وفي القلب منه زفرة ما تزايله
وكنت أعير الدمع قبلك من بكى ،
verses.ajuz: فأنت على من مات بعدك شاغله

translationEn: May the spring rains water a grave whose sides lie beyond Ghamra — the steady rain of spring and its heavy downpour. I lend them my hearing whenever grief is mentioned, and in my heart from that is a sigh that never leaves. I used to lend my tears to whoever wept before you — but now you have made me too occupied for whoever dies after you.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a grave, a tomb
steady spring rains, persistent drizzle of spring
its heavy rain, its downpour
a sigh, a deep breath of grief
never leaves it, never departs from it
to lend one's tears (to weep on behalf of others)

context: A very short but memorable elegy of three verses in which Al-Khansa prays for rain on Sakhr's grave and makes a striking observation: before his death she could weep for others, but now he occupies her grief so completely that she has no tears left for anyone else.

titleAr: كل امرىء باثا في الدهر مرجوم
titleEn: Every man who tarries in time is struck

verses.sadr: كل امرىء باثا في الدهر مرجوم

verses.ajuz: لا سوقة منهم يبقى ولا ملك
وقد أتاني حديث غير ذي طيل

verses.sadr: وليس يشمت من كانت له طوم
verses.ajuz: إن كان صخر تولى فالشمات بكم
قد كان صخرا جليدا كاملا برعا
فأصبح اليوم في رمس لدى جدث

verses.sadr: حمامة أو جرى في الغمر علجوم
verses.ajuz: تالله أنسى ابن عمرو الخير ما نطقت

translationEn: Every man who lingers in time will be struck down, and every tall-roofed house will be demolished. No commoner among them will remain, nor any king — neither those ruled by freemen nor those ruled by Romans. Calamities spare nothing in their path except God alone — and He whose root is firm is well known. A report has reached me — with no delay — from a people whose counsel has long been firm. That the morsel they told you of lodged behind the palate and could not be swallowed down. If Sakhr is gone, then the gloating is upon you — and one who has a mark cannot gloat. The relentless passage of calamities makes even the steadfast submit, and the fearful owl submits to them straight. Sakhr was steadfast, perfect, and skilled — tough in his resolve, raised up by the hard trials. But now he lies in a grave, in a tomb, with the soil heaped over him. By God, I shall never forget Ibn ʿAmr the good as long as a dove coos or a frog croaks in the flood. I speak of Sakhr — buried in the grave — but how can I conceal it when my tears pour down?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: struck, pelted — struck down by fate
firm, resolute in judgment (from Tihāma)
a morsel stuck in the throat — a calamity impossible to swallow
gullets, throats — places of swallowing
a mark, a scar — one who bears a mark cannot mock others
the steadfast, the hard-enduring man
a large frog (that croaks in the flood)

context: A poem combining wise meditation on the universality of death with elegy for Sakhr. The opening verses are hikma — no man, no house, no king escapes — before moving to rebuke those who might gloat, and then to personal grief expressed through natural images of the dove and frog.

titleEn: My soul is ransom for the Jashmite horseman
translationEn: My soul is ransom for the Jashmite horseman, and I would ransom him with all who are close to me. I would ransom him with every man of Banū Sulaym — both those who travel and those who dwell at home. I have singled out with this praise Qays, the brother of the free — a youth noble in a house of honor.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: ransom, sacrifice — giving oneself as ransom
the Jashmite — of the tribe of Jashm
a close relation, a dear one
those who travel, the nomadic faction
those who settle, the sedentary dwellers
a house of honor, a noble household

context: A short panegyric-elegy of three verses in which Al-Khansa declares her entire tribe and all her close kin as ransom for Qays, a horseman of the Jashm clan of Banū Sulaym, praising him as noble-born and honored.

titleAr: من لامني في حب كوز وذكره
titleEn: Whoever blames me for loving Kūz

verses.sadr: من لامني في حب كوز وذكره
verses.ajuz: فلاقى الذي لا قيت إذا حفز الرحم

verses.sadr: وثار غبار في الدهاس وفي الاكم
verses.ajuz: فيا حبذا كوز إذا الخيل أدبرت

verses.sadr: فنعم الفتى تعشوا إلى ضوء ناره
verses.ajuz: كويز بن صخر ليلة الريح والظلم

verses.sadr: وقد حال خير من أناس ورفدهم

translationEn: Whoever blames me for loving Kūz and remembering him — may he meet what I met when the womb-bond pressed hard. How wonderful is Kūz when the horses turned in retreat and dust rose in the soft sand and on the hills! What a fine youth — those who came guided by his fire in the night of wind and darkness were Kuwayiz ibn Sakhr. When the large fat she-camel pressed close to her calf and took shelter behind the clay-daubed ones and the lote-trees. The best of men provided for people and aided them with the two hands of a youth — neither miserly nor grudging.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: when kinship bonds press and urge (to grief)
soft sandy ground
guided themselves by the light (of a fire) in the dark
the large hump-backed mature she-camel
her trailing skirt-like tail hair, her calf-end
miserly, stingy
one who is bored, reluctant, grudging in giving

context: Al-Khansa elegizes Kūz (Kuwayiz ibn Sakhr, son of her brother Sakhr), praising him for his hospitality — travellers guided by his fire in dark stormy nights. The poem shows her grief extending to the next generation.

titleEn: By my life — and my life is no small thing

verses.sadr: وكان إذا ما أقدم الخيل بيشة

verses.ajuz: وكان ثمال الحي في كل ازمة

translationEn: By my life — and my life is no light thing — what a fine youth you have slain, O clan of Khathʿam. Both branches of Sulaym have been struck through him, and it is hard for us that he should be struck and we be humiliated. He used to advance his horses of Bīsha toward the plateau of al-Ashrāk, then halt and bridle them. Then he unleashed them plunging in squadrons like locusts swept by the wind of Najd toward Tihāma. By evening the hooves had worn smooth after him, and the pebbles used to clothe their back-heels in blood. They returned at nightfall with the plunder, each of them restless under the saddle-frame, leaner for the effort. And if the horses found no quarry to pursue at ʿĀqil or al-Rass, they pursued them at ʿAyyahuma. He was the support of the tribe in every time of hardship, their protection and the armored horseman. He rose to the heights when war tucked up her skirts — quenching her by force, or kindling her if he wished. I swore I would not cease letting a tear roll down — my two eyes turn it in their depth to let it stream.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a light matter, something trivial
you slew, you brought down (to ruin)
squadrons, groups of horses charging together
the back-heels of horses
the support of the tribe, the one who sustains them
the armored one, the one who rushes heedlessly into battle
tucked up her skirts — said of war when it becomes intense

context: Addressed to the clan of Khathʿam who killed a kinsman of Banū Sulaym, this elegy praises the fallen man's military skill in launching cavalry raids like swarms of locusts, and his role as tribal protector in hard times.

titleEn: Convey to Sulaym and her satiated kin
translationEn: Convey to Sulaym and those gorged with her bounty that we have surpassed them with the head of the great chief. We launched a raid upon them that quenched their thirst from the pool of poison. And ʿAbs we struck at dawn among their knolls — with a cup, but not the cup of wine. And Thaʿlaba of the alarm witnessed horses upon which were the lions of the thickets. They took shelter from us in fear of meeting — through striking and thrusting and excellent formation. We drove their riding-camels prostrate to their masters, both the loaded camels and those with mange.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the great chief, the leader
the pool of poison — the bitterness of defeat/death
their knolls, their elevated ground (place name/toponym)
lions of the thickets — fierce warriors
the battle formation, the ordered rank
mange — diseased camels driven off as spoils

context: Unlike most poems in this collection, this is a poem of tribal boasting (fakhr) in which Al-Khansa celebrates military victories over Sulaym, ʿAbs, and Thaʿlaba, describing raids and battle formations. It is one of the few non-elegiac poems in the batch.

titleEn: O eye, pour out your tears abundantly
translationEn: O my eye, be generous with tears — the pouring, the streaming tears — overflowing as when a string of pearls snaps and scatters through the thread of the stringing. Weep for Muʿāwiya the youth and the son of the great deep-natured men. The resolute one, builder of lofty glory upon the highest of pillars. His giving was abundant — at the moment of critical test he was never regretful. May God water his grave with the showers of an ever-drizzling cloud.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: pouring, streaming tears — tears that pour without ceasing
pearls, a string of pearls
the thread of the stringing
the deep, the vast-natured (epithet of noble lineage)
the great, the immense (in courage and generosity)
the resolute, the firm-judging
a continuous gentle drizzle, soft persistent rain

context: A brief elegy for Muʿāwiya, Al-Khansa's other brother, in mujzaʾ al-kāmil meter. The pearl-string simile for tears and the rain-prayer for the grave are classical elegiac motifs. Al-Khansa praises Muʿāwiya's resolute generosity and lofty ambition.

titleAr: أمن ذكر صخر دمع عينه يسجم
titleEn: Is it remembrance of Sakhr that streams

verses.sadr: أمن ذكر صخر دمع عينه يسجم

verses.ajuz: فتى كان فينا لم ير الناس مثله

verses.sadr: اذا كان يوم بالغا كل معظم
على نهج من طافح البحر خضرم
إذا قال فرسان اللقا : صخر أقدم
verses.ajuz: وأنت ابن فرع القوم يا صخر كلها

translationEn: Is it from the remembrance of Sakhr that your eye streams with rapid tears like a string of ordered pearls? A youth the likes of whom people never saw among us — a guardian for a mother or a trustee for one under his protection. A man of noble lineage through whom glory is reached by breadth and ease, and every tall proud man falls short of his excellence. You divided his two branches and were their unifying support when a day came reaching every great affair. Kinship bonds were never lost with you, and in what you administered and what you were entrusted with no criminal found shelter. Those who seek good at your hands are as if on a clear road from an overflowing sea. You made room for all needs, O Sakhr — and the one who perceived your generosity rushed toward it. You are the son of the branch of your people, O Sakhr, when the horsemen of the encounter say: Sakhr, advance! When my soul remembers his generosity and his valor, every ease and comfort is stripped from it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: streams, flows in drops (of tears)
like ordered/strung pearls — tears falling in regular drops
a guardian, a guarantor
one under sacred protection, a woman under guardianship
a tall, long-limbed proud man
the branch/summit of the people — the noblest
one who catches the scent, who perceives from afar

context: Al-Khansa catalogues Sakhr's virtues as guardian, trustee, arbiter, and man of battle. The poem uses legal and social language (guardian, trustee, kinship) alongside the battle-praise topos, ending with the powerful image of her soul stripped of all comfort when she recalls him.

titleAr: يا عين بكي على صخر لأشجان
titleEn: O eye, weep for Sakhr in grief

verses.sadr: يا عين بكي على صخر لأشجان

verses.ajuz: لو كان للدهر مال عند متلده

verses.sadr: آبي الهضيمة آت بالعظيمة متلاف م

translationEn: O my eye, weep for Sakhr from grief and a haunting thought hidden in the heart's core, a stored sorrow. The remembrance of Sakhr's generosity stirred me — the memory of one beloved, in sickness and grief. Weep for your brother on behalf of orphans whom the treachery of time has harmed, while all harm overwhelms me. Weep for the turbaned one, ornament of the two leaders, when spears were like ropes twisted together in their hands. And the son of al-Sharīd — his noble root was never matched in boasting by any great chief not of base origin. If time had wealth stored with its passing, then time's wealth would be Sakhr, the wealth of young men. He who spurned humiliation and brought the great deed, squandering noble gifts — no coward and no weakling. Protector of what was dear, bold-charging at the midday heat, rescuer of the captured herd, tough and never to be turned back twice. Climber of the watchtower, repeller of the sealed fortress, a seeker of the watering-place, a cutter of rivals. A witness at the councils, a bearer of banners, a cutter through valleys, a wolf of the open plain. He protected companions when battle grew fierce — those who said when generosity was measured out. He left his opponent's fingers yellowed — as though his two garments were splashed with saffron-dye. He gives you what a soul can barely relinquish from its ancestral wealth — generous, never holding back.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: from griefs, from sorrows (plural of شجن)
a haunting thought, an obsessive idea
the turbaned one — a leader who wears the turban of authority
one who refuses humiliation, who spurns subjugation
his fingers yellowed — from fear or from saffron-stained blood
ancestral wealth, inherited possessions
one who gives grudgingly and reminds of his gift

context: A rich praise-elegy in nineteen verses using a chain of honor-titles — protector, banner-bearer, council-witness, valley-crosser — each phrase dense with tribal values. The image of the opponent's fingers yellowed by the encounter is a powerful visual of Sakhr's ferocity.

titleAr: يا لهف نفسي على صخر وقد فزعت
titleEn: My soul aches for Sakhr when war alarmed

verses.sadr: يا لهف نفسي على صخر وقد فزعت

verses.ajuz: نعم الفتى أنت يوم الروع قد علموا

verses.sadr: عالي البناء إذا ما قصر الباني
كالليث في الحرب لا نكس ولا وان
verses.ajuz: حلف الندى وعقيد المجد اي فتى

translationEn: O how my soul aches for Sakhr when horses alarmed horses and equals met with equals in battle. Generous when men struck their fire-sticks to cast lots, free-handed and giving without reminding of his gift. A dignified noble man of pure character, swift-decisive in his passion, never greedy. Generous in his nature, lavish in his giving, a guardian of trust and never a traitor. What a fine youth you were on the day of alarm — they knew you to be a match when horsemen encircled horsemen. Of generous character, his qualities praised, tall in his structure when builders fell short. A shelter for widows and orphans when they hungered, a witness at councils, a feeder of guests. The sworn ally of generosity and the champion of glory — what a youth! — like the lion in battle, no coward and no weakling.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: was alarmed, was startled into action
generous, open-handed, easy in giving
dignified, grave, stately (of a man)
swift to decide, resolute, cutting through doubt
not greedy for the belly, not gluttonous
a witness at private councils, trusted with secrets
the sworn ally of generosity

context: A catalogue-elegy of eight verses listing Sakhr's qualities in parallel epithets. The density of honor-titles — generous, noble, pure-natured, swift, faithful, sheltering — makes this a representative example of Al-Khansa's praise-elegy style.

titleEn: My eye wept and its mote returned

verses.sadr: إذا ما الناب لم ترأم طلاها
لقد رزئت بنو عمر و فتاها
verses.ajuz: لئن جزعت بنو عمر و عليه

translationEn: My eye wept and its mote returned with a soreness that gives it no rest from weeping. For Sakhr — and what youth is like Sakhr when the old she-camel does not suckle her young? The youth of young men — none reached his extent, and when they reached their limit he was never spent. I swear by the Lord of the tawny camels urged to the Sacred House as their final destination. If Banū ʿAmr grieve over him, truly Banū ʿAmr have lost their youth. He had a hand that grips and a hand that milks — its moisture of generosity never dries. You see the noble-nosed great men of Sulaym dampening their beards with the moisture of their weeping. For a man of noble character who now lies in the belly of Ḥufayra, its echo loud. Let the best of Maʿad weep for Sakhr — those of forbearance and those of reason. And a force of cavalry I wrapped with a flank of cavalry — its millstone turning between its two rams. Lifting the excess of a smooth coat of mail upon a swift horse whose innards flutter. Running to meet the clash of the long spears with the cup of death at the moment of warming. Protecting and guarding when the burning of the thicket's heat repelled the people. He thrusts into it, setting it ablaze with a thrust — it takes in what both flanks contain when they clash. Who now is there for the guest when the north wind blows, shaking and dispersed, its east wind chasing it away? And its cold drove the lean she-camels crowding, backs humped, to the tent-walls, their sides exposed. There, had you descended upon the clan of Sakhr, the guest's hospitality would be fat from their camel humps. On the morning of Sakhr's death-notice I could not restrain the coursing tears that milked their flow. Your provider and your bearer you have left to lie where the grey earth's banks have crumbled. Let your people weep for you for lofty deeds and for battle — you were truly its youth. And a swift mare has lost you and so has found rest — would that her horseman could see her now.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: its mote, the speck in the eye — here the recurring pain of weeping
to suckle, to nurse (of a camel and her young)
to reach the end, to be exhausted (of a well or generosity)
the noble-nosed great men — leaders of pride and dignity
a smooth, well-made coat of chain mail
lean she-camels, camels that have dried up after calving
with backs arched and humped (from cold)

context: One of the longest and most elaborate elegies in this batch with 21 verses, this poem interweaves personal grief, cosmic witness (the great men of Maʿad), battle scenes, and hospitality imagery. The final verse — a war-horse finding rest without her rider — is one of Al-Khansa's most memorable closing images.

titleEn: Who has seen my two brothers for me
translationEn: Who has news of my two brothers for me — like two branches — or who has seen them both? Two brothers like two falcons — no eye has ever seen their like. Two great chiefs who never wronged each other, and whose sanctuary no one dared to violate. I weep for my two brothers — for the grave that has covered them both. No mature man like the two of them in the gathering, and no youth like their two youths. Two lances of Khatt in the middle of the sky — such is the shining of them both. They left no equal behind them when they departed, in lordship there is none to match them. They led without effort, freely, with the flow of their two generosities.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: had news of, perceived, found trace of
their equal, their match, one like them
two great chiefs, two dominant men
who never wronged each other
two lances of Khatt — famous Arabian lances of great quality
lordship, chieftaincy, honor
freely, naturally, without effort

context: A concise elegy for both brothers together, using dual forms throughout and the striking metaphor of two lances gleaming in the heart of the sky. Al-Khansa emphasizes that the two were matched and equal — in nobility, in never wronging each other, and in effortless generosity.

titleEn: O rooster calling at dawn

verses.ajuz: هلم كذا اخبرك ما قد بدا ليا
بدا لي أني قد رزئت بفتية
تعزيت واستيقنت ان لا اخا ليا

verses.sadr: وكيف ارجي العيش ضل ضلا ليا
verses.ajuz: كصخر بن عمر و خير من قد علمته

verses.sadr: وما لي لا أبكي على من لو انه
وغسان لم تسمع له الدهر لاحيا
verses.ajuz: وان تمس في قيس وزيد وعامر

translationEn: O rooster calling out at dawn — come here, let me tell you what has become clear to me. What has become clear is that I have been bereaved of young men — the remnant of a people who bequeathed me their weeping. When I heard the wailing women lament him, I sought consolation and became certain that I have no brother. A man like Sakhr ibn ʿAmr, the best I have ever known — how then can I hope for life? It has gone astray, astray from me. And why should I not weep for one who, had his day come before mine, would have wept for me? And were you to spend the night among Qays and Zayd and ʿĀmir and Ghassān — you would never hear anyone mourn him enough throughout all time.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the rooster calling at the pre-dawn hour
come, come here (an invitation)
I was bereaved, I suffered a great loss
the occasions of weeping, the sources of tears
the wailing women, the professional mourners
I sought consolation, I attempted to console myself
one who mourns adequately, one who gives him his due in lamentation

context: An unusual elegy that opens by addressing a rooster crowing at dawn — the pre-dawn hour associated with wakefulness and grief in Arabic poetry. Al-Khansa tells the rooster her sorrow, noting that Sakhr was so universally beloved that even the great tribes of Arabia could never fully mourn him.

titleAr: ارى الدهر افنى معشري وبني أبي
titleEn: I see time has destroyed my kin and my father's sons

verses.sadr: ارى الدهر افنى معشري وبني أبي

verses.ajuz: أيا صخر هل يغني البكاء أو الأسى
سقى الله ارضا اصبحت قد حوتهما

translationEn: I see that time has destroyed my companions and my father's sons, and I have become tearful, my weeping never drying. O Sakhr, does weeping or grieving avail anything for a dead man who has come to dwell in his grave? May God not send Sakhr and his covenant far away, and may God not send my Lord Muʿāwiya far away. May God not send Sakhr far, for he was the brother of generosity, building high deeds to lofty heights. I shall weep for them both, by God, as long as a she-camel lows in yearning and mountains stand firm as God has fixed them. May God water the land that has come to hold them both — with the pouring rains of the morning clouds.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: destroyed, annihilated, consumed utterly
tearful, one consumed by weeping (feminine form)
dwelling, remaining (in the grave)
lofty deeds, high achievements
a she-camel lowing with yearning for her lost calf
the firmly-fixed mountains, the stable peaks
the pouring, streaming rains

context: A meditation on total loss — time has destroyed all of Al-Khansa's people and both brothers are dead. She prays that God not distance them from His mercy, and swears to weep for as long as a she-camel calls for her calf and mountains remain fixed, ending with a rain-prayer for their graves.

titleAr: الا لا ارى في الناس مثل معاويه
titleEn: None among the people equals Muawiya

verses.sadr: الا لا ارى في الناس مثل معاويه
الا لا ارى كالفارس الورد فارسا

verses.ajuz: اذا شمرت عن ساقها وهي ذاكيه
بلينا وما تبلى تعار وما ترى
عليك بحزن ما دعا الله داعيه

translationEn: Truly I see no one among the people like Muʿāwiya when one of the nights strikes with a sudden calamity — a calamity at whose rustling even dogs prick their ears, and which comes out from the secret of the private council into open display. Truly I see no horseman like the roan horseman when boldness and conspicuous courage mount him. He was the clinging grip of war when it blazed up, when it tucked up its legs, burning with intensity. A leader of cavalry against cavalry as though they were jackals and eagles with cruel guardians upon them. We wear away while Taʿār and Yadhbul do not wear away — you do not see them changed by the events of time, remaining ever as they are. I have sworn that my tears and my wailing for you will not cease in grief for as long as God causes any caller to call.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: struck at night, came upon by night (of a calamity)
with a sudden calamity, a sudden disaster
its rustling, its faint sound
the roan horseman — a warrior on a roan-colored horse
the gripping clench of war — one who clings fast to battle
blazing, burning intensely (of fire or war)
fierce guardians, cruel angels — said of stern, merciless men

context: The final poem of this batch elegizes Muʿāwiya ibn ʿAmr, Al-Khansa's other brother, comparing him to the mountains Taʿār and Yadhbul that stand permanent while humans perish. He is celebrated as a roan-horseman of conspicuous courage and the clinging grip of war.
